stories.headline: 1 dead in suspected road rage shooting on South Broadway in Denver
stories.summary:
Denver police are investigating a homicide that may be connected to a road rage incident that occurred early Sunday morning in the area of Broadway and Maple. Officers responded to a shooting alert at 2:30 a.m. and located a victim who was transported to a hospital, where the person was later pronounced dead.

As of Sunday morning, investigators were still working to develop suspect information, according to a Denver Police Department social media post. No arrests have been announced.

Anyone with information about the shooting is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867. The case remains under active investigation.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs police shoot and kill man who fired at officers during traffic stop
stories.summary:
Three Colorado Springs police officers fatally shot a man late Saturday night after he fired at them during a confrontation in the 1200 block of North Academy Boulevard in East Colorado Springs. The officers were conducting proactive enforcement as part of a Metro Task Force when they approached a suspicious vehicle around 11 p.m.

After the officers identified themselves and told the man he was under arrest, the driver attempted to flee. When the vehicle became stuck, officers ordered both occupants to exit. The female passenger complied, but the male driver remained inside and fired at least one shot toward officers before being shot by three officers.

The man was pulled from the vehicle and given aid before being transported to a hospital, where he died. The El Paso County Sheriff's Office is leading the investigation, and Colorado law requires all body-worn camera footage to be released within 21 days.

stories.headline: 1 killed in 4-vehicle crash on I-25 in Greenwood Village
stories.summary:
One person was killed and an unknown number of others were injured in a four-vehicle crash Sunday morning on northbound Interstate 25 near the Arapahoe Road exit in Greenwood Village. The crash occurred around 6:30 a.m. and forced the closure of all northbound lanes except one for a period of time.

Police have not released further details on the cause of the crash or the identities of those involved. The Arapahoe County Coroner's Office will identify the deceased victim after next-of-kin notifications are completed.

An investigation into the cause of the crash is ongoing.

stories.headline: Bear Creek Fire prompts closure of Roxborough State Park; blaze under U.S. Forest Service management
stories.summary:
Roxborough State Park closed Sunday morning due to smoke and a potential wildfire threat from the nearby Bear Creek Fire, an 11-acre blaze burning in the Pike National Forest southwest of the park boundary. The fire was located approximately two miles northwest of Rampart Range Road and South County Highway 67, near the Indian Creek Campground in Douglas County.

West Metro Fire officials reported that the fire was moving slowly and not posing a significant threat as of Sunday afternoon. The U.S. Forest Service assumed management of the fire because it is burning on federal land. Investigators believe the fire may be human-caused given its proximity to hiking trails and a campground.

The nearby Sharptail Ridge Open Space was also temporarily closed Sunday due to the fire but reopened around noon, according to Douglas County officials. Increased air and ground crew activity was expected in the area as firefighting efforts continued.

stories.headline: Thousands attend 79th annual Easter Sunrise Service at Red Rocks Amphitheatre
stories.summary:
Between 8,000 and 10,000 worshippers gathered at Red Rocks Amphitheatre at dawn Sunday for the 79th annual Easter Sunrise Service, a Colorado tradition hosted by the Colorado Council of Churches since 1947. The free event began at 6 a.m. and featured music, prayer and sermons from local faith leaders as the sun rose over the Front Range.

Musical worship was led by Emerson Rose and the Cherokee Trail High School choir from the Cherry Creek School District. Kathy Escobar, co-founder of the Refuge Church in north Denver, delivered the sermon, emphasizing community and faith in action. Additional speakers included the Rev. Fernando Rodriguez and seminary professor Michelle Ferrigno Warren.

The Colorado Council of Churches represents 13 Christian denominations and more than 600 churches statewide. Donations collected beyond the cost of the event will be partially directed to the Food Bank of the Rockies.

stories.headline: Record-low snowpack is devastating southwestern Colorado agriculture and water supplies
stories.summary:
Colorado is experiencing its lowest statewide snowpack since record-keeping began in 1941, with levels at just 26 percent of the median as of last week, according to USDA National Water and Climate Center data. Conditions are even more extreme in southwestern Colorado, where the San Miguel-Dolores-Animas-San Juan River Basin sits at 19.5 percent of median and the Gunnison River Basin at 23 percent.

The Ute Mountain Ute Farm and Ranch Enterprise in Towaoc will operate on just 10 percent of its allocated Colorado River water this summer, planting only one of six sections of its agricultural land. The region was also hit by a heat dome in March that set numerous daily temperature records and accelerated snowmelt.

Gov. Jared Polis has activated the state's drought response through the Colorado Department of Natural Resources, including the Colorado Drought Task Force — a move last taken in 2020. Navajo Reservoir in the southwest currently sits at about 60 percent of capacity, roughly 50 feet below full pool.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance bipartisan bill to overhaul competency system after high-profile case
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are pushing a 197-page bipartisan measure, Senate Bill 149, that would create new mechanisms to institutionalize mentally ill or disabled defendants accused of serious crimes whose criminal cases cannot proceed due to incompetency findings. The effort follows a high-profile Weld County case in which a 21-year-old man with an intellectual disability had charges dismissed after being found incompetent, only to be arrested again weeks later for allegedly bringing a gun onto a college campus — a case that drew attention from Gov. Jared Polis and Elon Musk.

The bill would establish a clear legal pathway to keep such defendants confined and receiving treatment even after criminal charges must be dropped, and would also lower the threshold for involuntary mental health commitments. Supporters include major figures across Colorado's criminal justice system.

Mental health advocates and competency experts oppose the bill, warning it could lead to the inappropriate warehousing of people with widely varying needs. Jennifer Turner of Bridges of Colorado said the approach could create long-term problems by placing diverse individuals in the same facilities with the same care. The measure is sponsored in part by Sen. Judy Amabile, a Boulder Democrat.

stories.headline: Colorado faces $1.5 billion budget shortfall; Medicaid, substance abuse programs face deep cuts
stories.summary:
Colorado's Joint Budget Committee has completed work on a state budget proposal for fiscal year 2026-27 that must close a $1.5 billion general fund shortfall — the third major budget gap lawmakers have addressed in two years. The formal spending bill is expected to be unveiled this week and will total approximately $18.6 billion.

Among the cuts already agreed to: reduced overall Medicaid provider compensation, rejection of across-the-board state employee pay raises, higher tuition rates at state universities, and reductions to substance use disorder treatment programs. Lawmakers did set aside funds to cover rising employee health insurance costs to prevent take-home pay from shrinking.

Budget writers and analysts warn that Colorado's fiscal challenges will persist beyond the current session, driven largely by soaring Medicaid costs. Democrats have attributed the structural deficit in part to the constraints of the Taxpayer's Bill of Rights, while Republicans argue the majority party has expanded government beyond sustainable levels.

stories.headline: Update: JBS workers return to Greeley plant without new contract; union ends strike unconditionally
stories.summary:
Thousands of workers at the JBS meatpacking plant in Greeley will return to work Tuesday after the United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 ended its strike and made an unconditional offer for employees to return — without any new agreement in place. JBS confirmed Saturday it received formal notification from the union that the strike had ended.

"This decision by the union comes without any new agreement or change to the company's original offer," JBS said in a statement. The company said its last, best and final offer — which includes wage increases, a pension and other benefits — remains on the table and it hopes workers will soon have the opportunity to vote on it.

UFCW Local 7 President Kim Cordova said JBS has agreed to resume contract negotiations on Thursday and Friday, and vowed the fight would continue. Roughly 3,800 workers had been on strike since March 16 over pay and safety conditions. Some workers and community supporters expressed confusion and disappointment at the sudden end to the strike.

stories.headline: Rockies beat Phillies 4-1 behind Sugano's strong Coors Field debut, Moniak's two home runs
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies salvaged the finale of their home opening series Sunday with a 4-1 victory over the Philadelphia Phillies at Coors Field, improving to 3-6 on the season. Tomoyuki Sugano, signed to a one-year deal this offseason, delivered six strong innings, allowing just one run on four hits while striking out five — the Rockies' first quality start of 2026.

Mickey Moniak homered twice, including a solo shot in the first inning and another in the fifth off Phillies starter Taijuan Walker. T.J. Rumfield added a two-run home run in a three-run first inning; Rumfield is hitting .345 with two home runs and five RBI through his first nine games.

Victor Vodnik struck out the final batter with runners on base in the ninth to earn his first save of the season. Manager Warren Schaeffer praised Sugano's command and pitch mixing, noting the veteran adapted effectively despite not having his best split-finger fastball. The Rockies host the Houston Astros on Monday.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC draws 0-0 at Seattle Reign for second consecutive scoreless tie
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC played to a scoreless draw at Seattle Reign FC on Saturday, its second straight 0-0 result and third clean sheet of the season. The result leaves Summit FC with six points through five games and sitting ninth in the NWSL standings, tied with Bay FC and Kansas City Current.

Summit FC controlled much of the final 30 minutes after a difficult start to the second half, with substitute right back Ayo Oke injecting pace on the right wing after coming on in the 60th minute. Denver had eight shots on the night but was unable to convert, with Carson Pickett and Janine Sonis each coming close late in the match.

Goalkeeper Abby Smith, the NWSL goalkeeper of the month, made two crucial saves in the first half to keep the match scoreless. Coach Nick Cushing said he was satisfied with the six-point haul but acknowledged the team should have won both its most recent matches. Summit FC's next game is a home match against the San Diego Wave in three weeks.

stories.headline: Broncos' NFL Draft preparations in focus as team eyes linebacker, quarterback competition looms
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are deep in pre-draft preparations as the NFL Annual Meeting wrapped up in Phoenix, with team leadership including owner Greg Penner, general manager George Paton and president Damani Leech meeting with media. Analysts note the Broncos have identified linebacker as a top positional need heading into the draft.

Quarterback Bo Nix and cornerback Pat Surtain II have both responded publicly to the team's offseason trade for wide receiver Jaylen Waddle, signaling optimism about the upgraded receiving corps. The Broncos are also expected to hold an open quarterback competition this summer.

President Damani Leech highlighted that Empower Field at Mile High earned the top game-day experience ranking in the NFL, and noted that the team's on-field improvements are positively affecting its business goals heading into the 2026 season.

weather.notable: About 10 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: The big yellow one is the sun. I know that one because of the heat that comes off of it.

topStory.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes on Power Plants, Oil Seizure as Pilot Rescue Highlights Lingering Risks
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump claimed a major victory after the second crew member of a downed U.S. F-15 fighter jet was recovered from Iranian territory, declaring the rescue demonstrated "overwhelming air dominance and superiority." The operation involved establishing a forward airfield and refueling point inside contested Iranian territory, holding the position for several hours while damaged aircraft were destroyed and replacements dispatched.

Despite Trump's triumphant framing, defense analysts and Washington sources who spoke to the BBC described a more complicated picture. The loss of two aircraft and damage to at least one helicopter from hostile fire indicated that Iranian military capabilities remain intact in some form, even after weeks of heavy U.S. and Israeli strikes and Trump's earlier assertion that Tehran had "no anti-aircraft equipment" left. Particular concern centered on man-portable air defense systems, or Manpads, which are shoulder-launched missiles effective against low-flying aircraft and difficult to locate and destroy from the air.

Trump simultaneously issued sharp new threats against Iran over the weekend, warning in an expletive-laden post on Truth Social that the Iranian regime would be "living in Hell" if the Strait of Hormuz was not opened to navigation. He also told Fox News he might move to "take" Iran's oil and threatened to begin striking Iranian power plants and bridges if a diplomatic agreement was not reached, setting what he described as a self-imposed deadline for further escalation.

The president also struck a conciliatory note in separate remarks to reporters, suggesting a deal with Iran could be close at hand. Military planners have presented options to the White House that include an airborne or amphibious operation against Kharg Island, Iran's key oil export terminal, and potential seizure of highly enriched uranium stored at fortified underground sites, though the recent aircraft losses may complicate the calculus around such operations. Human rights organizations have warned that strikes on power plants and civilian infrastructure could constitute war crimes and cause widespread harm to Iranian civilians.

The conflicting signals from Washington leave the strategic direction of the conflict uncertain. Mediators are reportedly seeking a ceasefire, but no agreement has been announced. The outcome of the pilot recovery mission may cut both ways in internal deliberations — reinforcing either the risks of deeper military engagement or confidence in U.S. forces' ability to operate inside Iranian territory under hostile conditions.
